narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—pouring your heart into law school dreams only to have the door close can feel crushing, and I'm truly sorry you're facing this right now. Our prediction model looked at your profile and saw a 74% chance of struggling to pass the bar exam, which is why we had to make this tough call on your application. What stood out most was your LSAT score of 25.5—it was the biggest signal pulling toward difficulty ahead, as it's on the lower side compared to those who typically succeed. Layered on top was your Year 3 decile of 1, meaning you ranked in the bottom tenth of your peers that year, which strongly reinforced the model's concerns about sustaining strong performance. Your Year 1 decile of 2 wasn't far behind, showing early challenges that carried forward and painted a picture of consistent academic hurdles. Coming from a lower-middle family income bracket added another layer, as the model associates that with extra pressures that can impact focus and resources for bar prep. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.1, while solid in spots, didn't quite offset those trends, tipping things further toward the risk of failure. Even factors like being a white male pursuing full-time studies nudged the prediction in that direction, based on patterns we've seen in past outcomes. It's not that any one thing doomed your chances—it's this combination that worried us, like a storm cloud forming from gathering winds. You've got real potential here, and maybe targeting that LSAT or building those deciles higher could rewrite this story next time. Hang in there; this isn't the end of your path, just a detour to reflect and strengthen. We're rooting for you to turn it around.
